coredata.dc:title: A dual-potential ratiometric electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on Au@CDs nanoflowers, Au@luminol nanoparticles and an enzyme-free DNA nanomachine for ultrasensitive p53 DNA detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Li
Liu, Pengfei
Liu, Zhijun
Cao, Haixia
Ye, Shuying
Zhao, Kairen
Liang, Guoxi
Zhu, Jun-Jie
coredata.dc:description: Herein, an amplified electrochemiluminescence (ECL) ratiometric biosensor for p53 DNA sequence assay was described based on gold@carbon dots nanoflowers (Au@CDs), gold@luminol nanoparticles (Au@luminol) as luminophors and an enzyme-free DNA walker for signal amplification. The ratiometric strategy endowed the sensor with a prominent accuracy. Taking advantage of DNA-walker signal amplification feature, a high sensitivity for target detection was achieved. Under the optimal conditions, the designed biosensor exhibited a lower limit of 0.34 fM and wide linear range from 1 fM to 10 pM for p53 DNA sequence detection. In particular, compared with traditional Ru(bpy)3 3+/Ru(bpy)3 2+ derivatives and semiconductor nanoparticles, Au@luminol and Au@CDs have the advantages of cost-effective, excellent biocompatibility, and easy labeling, which offered new opportunity for building ratiometric ECL biosensor platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-potential
Ratiometric
Electrochemiluminescence
p53 DNA
DNA walker

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4, Au@CDs nanoflowers and Au@luminol nanoparticles
Feasibility of the developed biosensor for p53 DNA sequence detection
ECL and EIS characterization of the stepwise fabrication of the biosensor
Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the biosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Senior Talent Foundation of Jiangsu University
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) Au@luminol and (B) Au@CDs. Photoluminescence spectra of (C) luminol, Au@luminol and (D) CDs, Au@CDs.

(A) ECL responses of the biosensor at different experimental conditions. (B) EIS characterization for the stepwise fabrication of the ECL biosensor. (a, a’) bare GE, (b, b’) Au@luminol-HP1/GE, (c, c’) MCH/Au@luminol-HP1/GE, (d, d’) MCH/Au@luminol-HP1/GE incubated with 10 μL 1 pM walker DNA for 60 min, (e, e’) MCH/Au@luminol-HP1/GE incubated with 10 μL 1 pM DNA walker and 3 μL Au@CDs-HP2 for 60 min.

(A) ECL responses of the proposed biosensor incubated with different concentrations of p53 DNA sequence. (B) ECL intensities of anodic Au@luminol and cathodic Au@CDs corresponding to different concentrations of p53 DNA sequence. (C) Calibration curve for p53 DNA sequence. The error bars represent means ± SD (n = 3).

(A) Selectivity evaluation for the proposed ECL biosensor. (a) blank, (b) 10 pM non-complementary DNA, (c) 10 pM single-base-mismatched p53 DNA, and (d) 100 fM target p53 DNA sequence. (B) Reproducibility of the proposed biosensor. The concentration of p53 was 100 fM. The error bars represent means ± SD (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of the dual-potential ratiometric ECL biosensor for detection of p53 DNA.
The oligonucleotide sequences used in this work.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SM: Single-base mismatched sequence; NC: Non-complement sequence.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery results of p53 DNA added in human serum (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A dual-potential ratiometric electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on Au@CDs nanoflowers, Au@luminol nanoparticles and an enzyme-free DNA nanomachine for ultrasensitive p53 DNA detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-potential ratiometric electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on Au@CDs nanoflowers, Au@luminol nanoparticles and an enzyme-free DNA nanomachine for ultrasensitive p53 DNA detection.

Herein, an amplified electrochemiluminescence (ECL) ratiometric biosensor for p53 DNA sequence assay was described based on gold@carbon dots nanoflowers (Au@CDs), gold@luminol nanoparticles (Au@luminol) as luminophors and an enzyme-free DNA walker for signal amplification. The ratiometric strategy endowed the sensor with a prominent accuracy. Taking advantage of DNA-walker signal amplification feature, a high sensitivity for target detection was achieved. Under the optimal conditions, the designed biosensor exhibited a lower limit of 0.34 fM and wide linear range from 1 fM to 10 pM for p53 DNA sequence detection. In particular, compared with traditional Ru(bpy)3 3+/Ru(bpy)3 2+ derivatives and semiconductor nanoparticles, Au@luminol and Au@CDs have the advantages of cost-effective, excellent biocompatibility, and easy labeling, which offered new opportunity for building ratiometric ECL biosensor platform.

ce:section.ce:para: The p53 gene, a kind of tumor suppressor gene, plays an important role in controlling cell growth and modulating DNA repair processes and is well-known as “the guardian of genome” [1–3]. The mutations of p53 gene are closely related to about 50 % of human cancers and often occur in patients with early cancer progression and late-stage cancer metastasis [4]. So far, several analytical methods have been developed for p53 gene analysis, including polymerase chain reaction (PCR)-assisted semiautomatic electrophoresis [5], fluorescence [6,7], surface plasmon resonance [8], chemiluminescence [9], and electrochemistry [10]. However, most of them suffer from drawbacks such as lack of sufficient sensitivity and selectivity, cumbersome procedures, or the need for expensive equipment and high skills. Therefore, it is possible to further develop a simple, highly sensitive, and selective detection method for p53 gene detection.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) has received widespread attention in the field of bioanalysis due to its high selectivity, high sensitivity and simple equipment [11–13]. Although ECL technology has many advantages, most of the previously reported ECL methods are based on a single emission intensity change, which may cause false positive or negative results under variable external environment [14]. Ratiometric strategy is considered as an effective way to eliminate most such interferences by a built-in correction of the two emitter’s signals. At present, two main strategies have been adopted, namely the dual-wavelength ratiometric and the dual-potential ratiometric method to construct a proportional ECL biosensor [14–17]. However, for the dual-wavelength ratiometric strategy, the ECL intensity was reduced due to the use of essential optical filters or spectrometers. This makes them less sensitive than expected [18]. In contrast, the dual-potential methods would be more sensitive because they are independent of the spectrum difference of probes. The main issue in constructing a dual-potential ratiometric ECL sensor is to choose two ECL reporting units with potential-dependent characteristics [19]. Most of reported ratiometric ECL emitters are Ru(bpy)3 3+/Ru(bpy)3 2+ derivatives and semiconductor nanoparticles [14–17]. Ru-complexes are relatively expensive and difficult to label, and semiconductor nanoparticles may lead to the loss of the bioactivity, which limit their practical application [20,21]. Thus, it is of great significant to exploit novel ECL materials.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a novel kind of carbon-based ECL luminophore that can produce ECL at cathodic potential. [22–24]. Luminol is a well-known anodic emitter with hydrogen peroxide as coreactant to produce ECL at anodic potential [25,26]. Given the fact that CDs and luminol produce ECL signals at two different potentials, it is possible to achieve dual-potential ratiometric measurement based on the dual-signal of these two probes in one single scan. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report on the use of CDs-luminol for ratiometric ECL sensing. This may be due to the relatively weak luminous capability of two emitters [27]. Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) metallization represents an efficient approach to amplify the ECL response of probes owing to their large surface area, excellent electric conductivity and catalytic activity. For example, our group has developed high luminous Au NPs/carbon dots hybrids (Au@CDs) to enhance the ECL signal of CDs [28]. Many researchers applied Au NPs to improve the ECL performance of luminol [27,29]. Au@luminol and Au@CDs not only have excellent ECL activities and biocompatibility, but also have the merits of cost-effective and easy labeling, so they may offer new opportunities for the development of dual-potential ratiometric ECL biosensors.

In addition, with the development of DNA nanotechnology, the combination of analytical techniques and isothermal nucleic acid amplification strategies has become a research hotspot in highly sensitive bioassay area. Among the various isothermal nucleic acid amplification strategies, DNA walking nanomachines (also known as DNA walkers) are of particular interest because they are able to autonomously move along the pre-designed DNA tracks, thereby generating cascaded signal enhancement [30–33]. The motion of DNA walkers is usually propelled by DNAzyme/endonuclease-mediated hydrolysis and toehold-mediated strand displacement [33]. However, the protease/DNase reaction may be affected by many factors, such as temperature, pH, ion concentration and recognition sequence, which can affect walking efficiency and limit its application in complex biological systems and convenient biosensors [33]. In contrast, strand displacement is a promising alternative method for constructing DNA nanomachines that can achieve reliable motion without being disturbed by environmental factors [34]. Especially in 2016, Jung et al. developed a catalyzed hairpin assembly (CHA)-driven bipedal DNA walker by using a track hairpin DNA and a fuel hairpin DNA, which exhibits a fast response kinetic and a high walking efficiency [35]. Since then, a series of fluorescence and electrochemical methods have sought to expand the application of CHA-driven bipedal DNA walkers in sensing nucleic acids and proteins [36–39]. However, it is noteworthy that the application of CHA-driven bipedal DNA walkers in ECL biosensors is still in its infancy.

In this work, we developed a novel dual-potential ratiometric ECL biosensor for DNA detection based on the combination of Au@luminol nanoparticles, Au@CDs nanoflowers and a CHA-driven bipedal DNA walking nanomachine. In principle, the proposed ECL sensing protocol was particularly accurate and relatively simple due to the ratiometric strategy and enzyme-free design. Taking advantage of the high signal amplification capacity of DNA walker, a highly sensitive detection of p53 DNA with a detection limit at the sub-femtomolar level was achieved.

6-Mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH), luminol, hydroxylsuccinimide sodium salt (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO. USA). Other reagents were of analytical grade. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm, Millipore, MA) was used throughout the study. All oligonucleotides were provided by Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). And their sequences are displayed in Table 1 . TE buffer (10 mM Tris-HCl, 1 mM EDTA, pH 8.0) was utilized as solvent of the oligonucleotides.

A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100) was used to observe the morphology of the nanoparticles at an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were carried out on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, USA). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was accomplished by using a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai, China) with a classical three-electrode arrangement as follows: a modified gold electrode (GE, Φ =3 mm) as working electrode, Pt wire as counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) as reference electrode. The corresponding ECL signal data was carried out on a homemade ECL system, which is composed of a model CHI 660E workstation and an RFL-1 ultraweak chemiluminescence detector (Remax, Xian, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4, Au@CDs nanoflowers and Au@luminol nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: According to our previous works [28,40], Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared by a hydrothermal method; Au@CDs and Au@luminol nanoparticles were prepared by CDs and luminol directly reduction of HAuCl4 without any other reducer and stabilizer.

Different DNA solutions were prepared as follows. Fifty microliters of CP strand (10 μM) was mixed with 50 μL of walker strand (10 μM) and heated at 95 °C for 5 min, then slowly cooled down to 4 °C to obtain CP-walker duplexes. Other DNA sequences (target p53 DNA, HP1 and HP2) were kept at 95 °C for 5 min, then naturally cool down to room temperature separately, and stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

For the preparation of Fe3O4-(CP-walker), 100 μL of CP-walker (5 μM) were added to EDC/NHS activated Fe3O4 solution (50 μL, 10 mg/mL) and shaken gently at room temperature for 0.5 h. The synthesized Fe3O4-(CP-walker) was washed with water and re-dispersed in 150 μL TE buffer (pH 8.0).

Au@luminol-HP1 was fabricated through Au-S bond. Fifty microliters of HP1 (10 μM) was treated with 10 mM TCEP and incubated in the dark for 1 h to reduce disulfide bonds. Then, 1 mL of Au@luminol solution was added. The resulting solution was incubated at 37 °C for 12 h to form Au@luminol-HP1. The obtained Au@luminol-HP1 centrifuged, washed with water and re-suspended 1 mL of TE buffer. Similarly, Au@CDs-HP2 was also prepared.

The GE was polished with alumina slurries (0.3 and 0.05 μm) to obtain a mirror-like interface, and then washed ultrasonically in ethanol and ultrapure water. It was further electrochemically cleaned by scanning at a potential range from 0 to +1.5 V in H2SO4 (0.5 M) until a stable voltammetric peak obtained. Then, dropped 10 μL of Au@luminol-HP1 solution onto the cleaned GE and incubated for 1 h to form Au@luminol-HP1/GE. The modified electrode was further immersed in 1 mM MCH for 1 h to block the non-specific binding sites. The blocked electrode was washed twice with PBS (0.1 M, pH 8) and stored at 4 °C for the following experiment.

For detection, 5 μL of different concentrations of p53 DNA sequence was added in 5 μL of Fe3O4-(CP-walker) solution, and incubated at 37 °C for 50 min. Subsequently, the sample containing bipedal DNA walker was obtained through a single magnetic separation. Then, the obtained sample solution and 10 μL of Au@CDs-HP2 were dropped onto the biosensor surface at the same time and reacted at 37 °C for 1 h. After washing, the ECL of the biosensor was recorded in 0.1 M PBS (pH 8) containing H2O2 (100 mM) and K2S2O8 (100 mM). The potential scanning range was +0.8 to −1.6 V, and the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set at 800 V.

In order to characterize the prepared nanomaterials, we used TEM to observe their morphologies and sizes. As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, Au@luminol exhibited regular spherical shape with an average diameter of 20 nm and Au@CDs were flower-like nanoparticles with diameter around 50 nm. The nanoflower morphology of Au@CDs may be explained that CDs were functioned as matrix phase during the procedure of Au3+ salt reduction [41]. For Au@luminol, since there is no matrix phase, small spherical Au NPs are obtained. TEM image in Fig. S1 showed that the Fe3O4 magnet nanoparticles were well-dispersed and nearly spheres with an average diameter of 200 nm.

The successful preparation of Au@luminol and Au@CDs were further confirmed by PL spectra. As shown in Fig. 1C, compared with the spectrum of luminol, Au@luminol exhibited the same shape but higher intensity at 350−500 nm, which is consistent with the described data [42]. On the contrary, a lower PL of Au@CDs than CDs was observed, which is attributed to the photo-induced electron transfer from CDs shell to the Au core (Fig. 1D) [43].

The principle of the ECL biosensor proposed was illustrated in Scheme 1 . First, the CP-walker duplexes were modified on the surface of Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticle via amido bond. When p53 DNA sequence is present, it could hybridize with the CP and release the walker strand from the Fe3O4-(CP-walker) composites through the toehold-mediated strand displacement reaction (Scheme 1A). After magnetic separation, the disengaged walker strand as well as Au@CDs-HP2 were cast on MCH/Au@luminol-HP1/GE to produce dual-potential ECL signals and amplify the signals. Specifically, as shown in Scheme 1B, the “legs” of walker strand could hybridize with overhanging toehold and the stem of Au@luminol-HP1 and unfold the HP1. The exposed HP1 could unfold the HP2 and hybridize with the Au@CDs-HP2, leading to the release of the walker strand. The released walker strand catalyzed the next cycle. N cycles later, large amounts of Au@CDs-HP2/Au@luminol-HP1 duplexes formed on the surface of electrode. As a result, an obvious Au@CDs signal was observed, while the ECL signal of Au@luminol decreased due to a dramatic increment of steric hindrance, allowing to the sensitive ratiometric detection of target p53.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the developed biosensor for p53 DNA sequence detection
ce:section.ce:para: The whole sensing strategy could be simply divided into two modules: 1) release the bipedal DNA walker through toehold-mediated strand displacement of CP-walker duplexes and target; 2) bipedal DNA walker’s movement on the surface of the electrode driven by CHA.

The target p53 DNA triggered DNA walker releasing from CP-walker duplexes was first verified by the 12 % native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) in Fig. S2. Lane 1 and lane 2 were the bands of CP and walker strand, respectively. A low migration band in lane 3 was the CP-walker duplexes, while another band belonging to walker is also observed in lane 3 owing to the twice amount than CP. With the subsequent addition of p53 DNA (lane 4), the band of CP-walker disappeared, and a faster migration rate was observed because the walker in a CP-walker duplexes was displaced by p53 DNA through toehold-mediated strand displacement. Lane 5 was the pure CP-p53 DNA duplex. The results confirmed the successful release of walker strand from CP-walker duplexes.

In the case of the bipedal DNA walker’s movement, because the sequences of HP1, HP2 and walker legs used in this work were completely consistent with the previously reported study [35], the PAGE analysis of their reaction was, therefore, not performed. Instead of that, to evaluate if the DNA walker could work on the interface of electrode, the response of modified electrode with/without the addition of DNA walker strand was analyzed. As shown in Fig. S3, only an anodic ECL signal at the potential of +0.69 V (Au@luminol) was observed in the absence of DNA walker strand (curve a). The reason for this phenomenon was ascribed to that no Au@CDs was introduced onto the electrode surface. By contrast, in the presence of DNA walker strand, “legs” sequences of walker hybridized to electrode-bound Au@luminol-HP1 hairpins. This leads to strand exchange and opening of HP1, exposing the toehold sequence of HP1, and allowing the toehold sequence to interact with Au@CDs-HP2 in solution, and finally forming the thermodynamically favorable Au@luminol-HP1/Au@CDs-HP2 duplex on the surface of electrode and releasing the DNA walker. Therefore, an obvious cathodic ECL signal belonging to Au@CDs (−1.5 V) was observed. Meanwhile, a considerable decrease of anodic ECL signal (Au@luminol) occurred (curve b). It was possibly attributed to that introducing a large number of Au@CDs on electrode interface resulted in a large steric-hindrance effect which possibly hinders the reaction between the Au@luminol and H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL and EIS characterization of the stepwise fabrication of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL characterization of the sensor was shown in Fig. 2 A. The bare GE had almost no ECL signal (curve a) while a remarkable anodic ECL was observed after the Au@luminol-HP1 was added onto the electrode (curve b). Blocked with MCH induced a slightly decrease of Au@luminol anodic ECL (curve c). After incubation with the DNA walker, the ECL signal of Au@luminol had no significant change (curve d). However, an obvious cathodic ECL signal was observed (curve e) after further adding Au@CDs-HP2 because CHA occurred that make the Au@CDs immobilized onto the electrode to produce a signal. At the same time, the anodic ECL intensity of Au@luminol was greatly reduced due to a dramatic increment of steric hindrance.

In addition, EIS was implemented to characterize the stepwise preparation of the ECL biosensor [44,45]. As depicted in Fig. 2B, an extremely small semicircle was observed from the bare GE (curve a’), due to the electroactive ions being rapidly transported to the electrode interface. A bigger semicircular region was observed at the Au@luminol-HP1/GE owing to the repulsive electrostatic interaction between the Fe(CN)6 3−/4− and negatively charged HP1 (curve b’) [46]. The electrode blocked with MCH resulted in a significant increase in semicircle (curve c’) due to the formation of more compact layer that hinders the interfacial electron transfer [47]. The hybridization of walker “legs” with HP1 further restricted the migration of Fe(CN)6 3−/4− to the electrode, leading to a further increased semicircle diameter (curve d’). Finally, after modifying the electrode with Au@CDs-HP2, the semicircular area further increased due to HP1-HP2 duplex formed (curve e’). All these results indicated that the ECL biosensor was successfully fabricated as expected.

In order to gain the best performance of the biosensor, some conditions, including the pH value, concentration of coreactants, the target incubation time, and the walking time, have been comprehensively studied and optimized (see Fig. S4 for details). Under the optimized conditions, ratiometric ECL detection of p53 DNA is achieved. As shown in Fig. 3 A and B, when the concentration of p53 DNA increased from 0 to 10 pM, the cathodic ECL intensity of Au@CDs enhanced, while the anodic ECL intensity of the Au@luminol gradually decreased. There was a good linear relationship between the logarithm of Ic/Ia (where Ic is the cathodic ECL signal and Ia is the anodic ECL signal) and the logarithm of the target concentration in the range from 1 fM to 10 pM (Fig. 3C). The linear regression equation is lg(Ic/Ia) = 3.3970lgC + 0.2884 (R = 0.9952). Importantly, the proposed biosensor exhibited a lower detection limit of 0.34 fM (3S/N) than most reported analytical technologies (Table S1), which could well meet the requirement of target p53 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the developed biosensor, non-complementary DNA and single-base-mismatched p53 DNA were chosen as interfering agents. The results were shown in Fig. 4 A. It was noted that the highest Ic/Ia value could only be observed when p53 DNA (100 fM) was present; while the Ic/Ia values of the interfering substances (10 pM) were negligible, similar to the blank value. These results demonstrated that the proposed biosensor displayed an excellent selectivity toward p53 DNA. Moreover, the reproducibility was studied by employing ten repetitive measurements of the target under the same condition. The corresponding results were exhibited in Fig. 4B. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.2 % for intra-assay and 4.4 % for inter-assay indicated a satisfactory reproducibility. The stability of the DNA biosensor was also monitored by storing it at 4 °C and measured every one week. It can be seen that even after 5 weeks, the anodic signal remained at 99.97 % of its initial value, and the cathodic ECL signal remained at 86.04 % of its initial value (Fig. S5), indicating that the proposed biosensor has good stable performance.

In order to assess the effectiveness of the ECL DNA biosensor in clinical application, recovery experiments were performed in diluted human serum sample. In a first step of the assay, 100 μL of healthy human serum sample (obtained from the Jiangsu University Staff Hospital, China) was diluted in 900 μL PBS. After that, five different concentrations of p53 DNA were added into the diluted human serum, and were then tested using the proposed biosensor. The obtained recoveries in the range of 94.2 %−108.3 % were listed in Table 2 , and the RSD values were from 2.4 %−5.2 %, denoting that the proposed ECL biosensor could effectively assay p53 DNA in complicated samples.

In summary, a dual-potential ratiometric ECL biosensor was designed for the selective and sensitive detection of p53 DNA based on Au@luminol, Au@CDs, and an enzyme-free DNA walker. The prepared Au@luminol and Au@CDs nanomaterials not only have excellent ECL activities and biocompatibility, but also have the merits of cost-effective and easy labeling, so they may offer new opportunities for the development of sensitive ECL biosensors. The ratiometric strategy endowed the sensor with a prominent accuracy. Taking advantage of target-triggered DNA-walker signal amplification feature, a wide linear range and ultrahigh sensitivity for p53 DNA detection were achieved. This developed biosensor exhibited excellent selectivity and was successfully applied to detect p53 DNA in human serum samples, providing new opportunities for the development of a reliable method for biomedical research and early clinical diagnostics.

Li Wang: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Funding acquisition. Pengfei Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Software, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Zhijun Liu: Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Haixia Cao: Writing - review & editing. Shuying Ye: Writing - review & editing. Kairen Zhao: Writing - review & editing. Guoxi Liang: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Jun-Jie Zhu: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053) and Senior Talent Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 10JDG052, 10JDG038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128890.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current strategies to target p53 in cancer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Chen, W.G. Wang, W.S. El-Deiry, Current strategies to target p53 in cancer, Biochem. Pharmacol. 80 (2010) 724-730. https://doi:10.1016/j.bcp.2010.04.031

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A chimeric p53 evades mutant p53 transdominant inhibition in cancer cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Okal, M. Mossalam, K.J. Matissek, A.S. Dixon, P.J. Moos, C.S. Lim, A chimeric p53 evades mutant p53 transdominant inhibition in cancer cells, Mol. Pharm. 10 (2013) 3922-3933. https://doi.org/10.1021/mp400379c.
K.N. Schaefer, J.K. Barton, DNA-mediated oxidation of p53, Biochemistry 53 (2014) 3467-3475. https://doi.org/10.1021/bi5003184.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive and selective detection of the p53 gene based on a triple-helix magnetic probe coupled to a fluorescent liposome hybridization assembly via rolling circle amplification

Li, Sensitive and selective detection of the p53 gene based on a triple-helix magnetic probe coupled to a fluorescent liposome hybridization assembly via rolling circle amplification, Analyst 142 (2017) 3598-3604. https://doi.org/10.1039/c7an01255a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Elevated levels of wild-type p53 induced by radiolabeling of cells leads to apoptosis or sustained growth arrest

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective bifunctional oligonucleotide probe for homogeneous parallel fluorescence detection of protein and nucleotide sequence

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Programmable nanoassembly consisting of two hairpin-DNAs for p53 gene determination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, J. Feng, Z. Tan, H. Wang, Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy aptasensor for ultrasensitive detection of adenosine with dual backfillers, Biosens. Bioelectron. 60 (2014) 218-223. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2014.04.022.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Wang is currently working at Department of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University. Her current research interests include multifunctional nanomaterial and their applications in diagnosis, metastasis, and treatment of cancer.

Pengfei Liu is presently pursuing his master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. His current research focuses on biosensors and their applications in cancer cell research and drug evaluation.

Zhijun Liu is presently pursuing her master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. Her main research interest regards functional nanomaterials for biomedical and diagnostic applications.

Haixia Cao is presently pursuing her master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. Her current research focuses on biosensors and their applications in cancer cell research and drug evaluation.

Shuying Ye presently pursuing her master degree in environmental engineering in Jiangsu University. Her research interests include development of biosensors and advanced materials for various purposes.

Kairen Zhao is presently pursuing his master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. His research interests include development of biosensors and advanced materials for various purposes.

Guoxi Liang received his Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University. He is currently working at Department of Environment Science, Jiangsu University. His current research interests include nanobiosensor and nanofabrication.

Jun-Jie Zhu is a professor of analytical chemistry at School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University. His research focuses on preparation and characterization of nanomaterials, bioelectrochemistry and nanoelectrochemistry.
